region_of_origin: Europe (primarily France and USA for commercial production)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of prunes is 4–5 pieces (approximately 40g), providing around 96 kcal and 15.3g of sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Prunes, uncooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168162); cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo database (F04010035)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore database (F04010035) provides primary values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron. USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 168162) used to supplement saturated fat, potassium, vitamin C, vitamin K, and other micronutrients. Values are consistent between both sources within rounding tolerance.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for prunes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 29 (low) for prunes is documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Foster-Powell et al. (2002) Am J Clin Nutr 76:5–56. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (29) × carbohydrate per serving (40g × 63.88g/100g = 25.55g) / 100 = 7.4. Note: GL per serving = 29 × 25.55 / 100 = 7.4. Revised GL = 7.4 (see notes). Despite high total sugar, the low GI is attributed to the sorbitol content and high dietary fibre which slow glucose absorption.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar content (38.1g/100g) exceeds the 10g/100g solid-food sugar threshold for hypertension management; sodium is negligible at 2mg/100g but concentrated sugar drives insulin-mediated sodium retention
hypertension.guidance: Limit prune intake to no more than 3–4 pieces (approximately 30g) per occasion; despite exceptional potassium content (732mg/100g) which supports blood pressure regulation, the concentrated sugar load from drying overrides cardiovascular benefit at larger portions and fresh plums are preferred for hypertension management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Total sugar at 38.1g/100g far exceeds the 20g/100g diabetes sugar threshold; however, GI is low (29) due to sorbitol and high dietary fibre (7.1g/100g), meaning portion control is critical
type2_diabetes.guidance: Restrict to 2–3 prunes maximum per occasion (approximately 20–25g) and pair with protein or healthy fat to further blunt the glycaemic response; the low GI of 29 provides some mitigation but the concentrated sugar load makes prunes unsuitable as a free food for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Prunes are very low in saturated fat (0.09g/100g), contain no cholesterol, and provide high potassium (732mg/100g), dietary fibre (7.1g/100g), and polyphenol antioxidants (chlorogenic and neochlorogenic acids) which support cardiovascular health; the high sugar content is the primary concern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Consume in small portions of 3–4 prunes (approximately 30g) as part of a balanced diet; the polyphenol content, high potassium, and soluble fibre support cardiovascular health, but excessive intake should be avoided due to the high concentrated sugar content which can contribute to elevated triglycerides.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich in anti-inflammatory polyphenols (chlorogenic acid, neochlorogenic acid), high dietary fibre (7.1g/100g), and negligible saturated fat; evidence supports bone density maintenance relevant to RA patients on corticosteroids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Limit to 3–4 prunes per serving to avoid excessive sugar intake which can promote inflammatory mediators at high doses; prunes have documented evidence for bone density support which is particularly beneficial for RA patients on long-term corticosteroid therapy.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prunes are dried European plums (Prunus domestica) that have undergone natural dehydration to produce a dark, wrinkled fruit with a sweet, intensely flavoured, and chewy texture. The drying process concentrates all nutrients and sugars significantly compared to fresh plums. Prunes are a globally consumed dried fruit with particular prominence in European, American, and Middle Eastern food traditions, and are widely available in Singapore and Malaysia as a snack and culinary ingredient. Nutritionally, prunes per 100g provide 240 kcal, 2.18g protein, 0.38g fat, 63.88g carbohydrate, 38.13g sugar, and 7.1g dietary fibre. They are exceptionally rich in potassium (732mg/100g), which supports blood pressure regulation, and contain meaningful amounts of calcium (43mg), iron (0.93mg), magnesium (41mg), and B vitamins. Prunes are well known for their digestive health benefits, attributed to their high sorbitol content and dietary fibre, which promote bowel regularity. Despite a very high total sugar content, prunes have a low glycemic index of 29, attributed to the sorbitol and soluble fibre content that slow glucose absorption. Clinically, prunes are unsuitable as a free food for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension due to concentrated sugar, but small portions of 2–4 prunes can be incorporated with appropriate dietary management. Prunes are rich in polyphenol antioxidants including chlorogenic and neochlorogenic acids, which have anti-inflammatory properties relevant to conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. They are naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, with no allergens of concern.
common_user_queries: How many prunes should I eat per day?
Are dried plums and prunes the same thing?
What is the glycemic index of prunes?
Are prunes good for blood pressure?
Do prunes help with bone density?
Are prunes suitable for a low-sugar diet?

confidence_reason: Core nutritional values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (F04010035, confidence 0.95) with supplementary micronutrient data from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168162). Both are approved primary sources. GI value of 29 is directly documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. All values are internally consistent and cross-validated.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.09g/100g), monounsaturated fat (0.05g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (0.06g/100g), trans fat (0.0g/100g), potassium (732mg/100g), vitamin C (0.6mg/100g), vitamin A (39mcg RAE/100g), B vitamins, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water content sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 168162 (Plums, dried (prunes), uncooked) to supplement HPB primary values. 2. Cholesterol is 0.0mg/100g (confirmed zero — plant-based food contains no cholesterol by nature). Vitamin B12 is 0.0mcg/100g (naturally zero — plant-based food). Vitamin D is 0.0mcg/100g (naturally zero for dried fruit with no UV exposure). Trans fat is 0.0g/100g (naturally zero — unprocessed plant food). 3. Glycemic load per serving recalculated: GI 29 × (40g serving × 63.88g carbohydrate/100g = 25.55g carbohydrate) / 100 = 7.4. The gi_source field notes GL as 7.4; the glycemic_load_per_serving field is set to 7.4. 4. food_subgroup corrected from 'Fresh fruit' (input error) to 'Dried fruit' as prunes are dehydrated plums. 5. The 'wholegrain' dietary tag from the input has been removed as it is not applicable to dried fruit. 6. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI and SNOMED CT code are cited as defensible matches based on published ontology documentation; reviewers should verify FoodEx2 code A00LC against the current EFSA FoodEx2 browser if this record is used in EU regulatory contexts. 7. potassium_mg value of 732mg/100g is referenced in the input health_suitability section and corroborated by USDA SR Legacy data (732mg/100g for prunes uncooked).
